options.A: The condition p(a) = p(b) is necessary and sufficient for (*)
options.B: The condition p(a) = p(b) is necessary but not sufficient for (*)
options.C: The condition p(a) = p(b) is sufficient but not necessary for (*)
options.D: The condition p(a) = p(b) is not necessary and not sufficient for (*)

questions.workedSolution: Ifp(a) = p(b) = c, say, thenifthegraphofy = p(x)(strictly)increasesasitleaves(a,c)towards x = b, but then it must decrease again to get back to (b,c), so there must be a stationary point between x = a and x = b. Similarly, if the graph (strictly) decreases as it leaves (a,c), it must then increase again, which also results in a stationary point. The only other possibility is that the graph of y = p(x) is constant, in which case p′(x) = 0 throughout. In all cases, (*) holds, so p(a) = p(b) is sufficient for (*). However, it is not necessary for (*): if p(x) = x2 and a = −1, b = 2, then this satisfies (*) (with c = 0) but p(a) ̸= p(b). Thus the correct option is C.

questions.text: Consider the following statements about a polynomial f(x): I f(x) = px3 +qx2 +rx+s, where p = 0. II There is a real number t for which f(t) = 0. III There are real numbers u and v for which f(u)f(v) < 0. Which of these statements is/are sufficient for the equation f(x) = 0 to have a real solution? Statement I is Statement II is Statement III is sufficient sufficient sufficient
questions.workedSolution: I Every cubic crosses the x-axis, so this condition is sufficient for f(x) = 0 to have a real root. This also follows from III below if we take u to be a very large positive number and v to be a very large negative number. II Consider, for example, f(x) = x2+1. This quadratic has f′(x) = 2x so f′(0) = 0, but there is no real x for which x2+1 = 0. So this condition is not sufficient. III As f(u)f(v) < 0, we must have f(u) < 0 and f(v) > 0 or the other way round; either way, the graph of y = f(x) has values above and below the x-axis. Since f(x) is a polynomial, it must therefore cross the x-axis, and so the equation f(x) = 0 has a real root. (If we did not know that f(x) was a polynomial, this would not be sufficient; consider f(x) = 1 for x example.) The correct option is therefore C.

questions.text: The first seven terms of a sequence of positive integers are: u = 15 1 u = 21 2 u = 30 3 u = 37 4 u = 44 5 u = 51 6 u = 59 7 Consider the following statement about this sequence: (*) If n is a prime number, then u is a multiple of 3 or u is a multiple n n of 5. What is the smallest value of n that provides a counterexample to (*)?
questions.workedSolution: To be a counterexample, we need the antecedent (the ‘if’ part) to be true but the consequent (the ‘then’ part) to be false. n n is prime a multiple of 3 or 5 n 1 false – 2 true true 3 true true 4 false – 5 true false 6 false – 7 true false Thereforen = 5andn = 7arebothcounterexamples, andthesmallestcounterexampleisn = 5, which is option E.

questions.text: A student attempts to solve the following problem, where a and b are non-zero real numbers: Show that if a2 −4b3 ≥ 0 then there exist real numbers x and y such that a = xy(x+y) and b = xy. Consider the following attempt: (x−y)2 ≥ 0 (I) so x2 +y2 −2xy ≥ 0 (II) so (x+y)2 −4xy ≥ 0 (III) so x2y2(x+y)2 −4x3y3 ≥ 0 (IV) so a2 −4b3 ≥ 0 (V) Which of the following best describes this attempt?

options.B: It is incorrect, but it would be correct if written in the reverse order.
options.C: It is incorrect, but the student has correctly proved the converse.
options.D: It is incorrect because there is an error in line (II).
options.E: It is incorrect because there is an error in line (III).
options.F: It is incorrect because there is an error in line (IV).

questions.workedSolution: We start by checking that each step works. Line (II) follows from line (I) by expanding the brackets. The algebraic expression in line (III) expands as x2+2xy+y2−4xy, so it follows from line (II). Line (IV) follows from line (III) by multiplying by x2y2. Since x2y2 ≥ 0, the inequality keeps the same direction, so this is a valid deduction. However, how does line (V) follow from line (IV)? It assumes that a = xy(x+y) and b = xy in order to make the deduction. This argument has therefore shown that if a = xy(x+y) and b = xy, then a2 −4b3 ≥ 0, or even more explicitly: if there exist real numbers x and y such that a = xy(x+y) and b = xy, then a2−4b3 ≥ 0. However, the problem posed is the converse: ‘show that if a2−4b3 ≥ 0, then there exist real numbers x and y such that a = xy(x+y) and b = xy. So this attempt has proved the converse, though the argument would have been slightly better presented if the student had explicitly stated ‘assume that x and y are real numbers with a = xy(x+y) and b = xy’. The correct option is therefore C.

questions.text: Which of the following statements about polynomials f and g is/are true? I If f(x) ≥ g(x) for all x ≥ 0, then x f(t)dt ≥ x g(t)dt for all x ≥ 0. 0 0 II If f(x) ≥ g(x) for all x ≥ 0, then f(x) ≥ g(x) for all x ≥ 0. III If f(x) ≥ g(x) for all x ≥ 0, then f(x) ≥ g(x) for all x ≥ 0.
questions.workedSolution: x I Wecanthinkoftheintegral f(t)dtasbeingthe(signed)areaunderthegraphofy = f(x) from 0 to x. Since f(x) ≥ g(x) for all x ≥ 0, the area under the graph of y = f(x) will also be greater than that under the graph of y = g(x), so this statement is true. You might be concerned about values of x for which g(x) is negative. We could therefore demonstrate this algebraically as well. We have x x x f(t)dt− g(t)dt = (f(t)−g(t))dt. 0 0 0 Since f(t)−g(t) ≥ 0 for all x ≥ 0, we are integrating a non-negative function, and so the x x integral must be non-negative, that is to say, we must have f(t)dt ≥ g(t)dt. 0 0 II It is not clear why f(x) ≥ g(x) should imply that the gradient (derivative) of f(x) is greater than that of g(x), and it is relatively straightforward to find a counterexample. Here is one: y f(x) = (x−1)2 g(x) = 0 x In this example, f(x) ≥ g(x) for all x, but f′(0) < 0 while g′(x) = 0 for all x. III This is not necessarily true, as we could add a constant to g(x) without changing g′(x). For example, we could have f′(x) = g′(x) = 1 for all x, but f(x) = x while g(x) = x+1, so f(x) < g(x) for all x. The correct option is therefore B.

questions.text: A region R in the (x,y)-plane is defined by the simultaneous inequalities y −x < 3 y −x2 < 1 Which of the following statements is/are true for every point in R? I −1 < x < 2 II (y −x)(y −x2) < 3 III y < 5
questions.workedSolution: Manipulating inequalities algebraically requires care. For example, we cannot (in general) mul- tiply two inequalities together and obtain a valid inequality (which is what statement II has done). Neither can we subtract two inequalities (which is where statement I comes from). Inthiscase, oneapproachistosketchtheregionR describedbythepairofinequalitiesandthen determine whether the inequalities in statements I–III are satisfied in the whole of R. Another approach is to search for counterexamples for each statement, that is, values of x and y that do not satisfy the statement but do satisfy the original inequalities. This, however, might be harder to do, and if the inequalities are true, it may take a while to discover this. So let us sketch the given inequalities. The inequality y −x < 3 can be rearranged as y < x+3, so it is the region below y = x+3. Similarly, y −x2 < 1 can be rearranged as y < x2 +1, so it is the region below the quadratic y = x2+1. The region R is therefore the shaded area shown here (excluding the boundaries): y x We can now consider the three statements. I It is clear that this is not true in general, as the region R extends in all directions. For example, the point (10,0) lies in R (it is easy to check that this satisfies the original inequalities: 0−10 < 3 and 0−102 < 1) but x = 10 does not satisfy −1 < x < 2. II If we take x = 0, the inequality becomes y2 < 3, but (0,−2) is in R and (−2)2 = 4. So this statement is not true for all points in R. III We could take, for example, (6,6) as a point in R with y ≥ 5, so this statement is not true in general. The correct option is therefore A.

questions.text: Consider the following simultaneous equations, where p is a real number: p2 x +log y = 2 2 2 x +log y = 1 2 What is the complete range of p for which these simultaneous equations have a real solution (x,y)?
questions.workedSolution: Writing X = 2x and Y = log y gives the simultaneous equations pX +Y = 2 X +Y = 1 Subtracting gives (p−1)X = 1, so as long as p ̸= 1, we have X = 1 and so Y = p−2. p−1 p−1 Now the equation log y = p−2 gives a real value for y for any value of p ̸= 1 (specifically 2 p−1 y = 2(p−2)/(p−1)), but the equation 2x = 1 has a solution if and only if 1 > 0, which is if p−1 p−1 and only if p > 1. Therefore the original simultaneous equations have a real solution (x,y) if and only if p > 1, which is option C.

questions.text: A circle has equation x2 +ax+y2 +by +c = 0 where a, b and c are non-zero real constants. Which one of the following is a necessary and sufficient condition for the circle to be tangent to the y-axis?
questions.workedSolution: Approach 1 We start by completing the square to find the centre and radius of the circle: (x+ 1a)2−(1a)2+(y+ 1b)2−(1b)2+c = 0 2 2 2 2 which gives (x+ 1a)2+(y+ 1b)2 = (1a)2+(1b)2−c 2 2 2 2 so the circle has centre (−1a,−1b) and radius (1a)2+(1b)2−c. 2 2 2 2 Now let us sketch a circle tangent to the y-axis: y 0 x We can see that the circle is tangent to the y-axis if and only if its radius is equal to the distance of its centre from the y-axis. This is not the same as saying the radius is equal to the x-coordinate of the centre, as the centre may have a negative x-coordinate. As an equation, this condition is then (1a)2+(1b)2−c = −1a 2 2 2 which, on squaring, is equivalent to (1a)2+(1b)2−c = (1a)2 2 2 2 (This is equivalent to the previous equation because both sides of the first equation are non- negative.) Subtracting (1a)2 from both sides gives 1b2 −c = 0 or b2 = 4c, so this condition is necessary 2 4 and sufficient for the circle to be tangent to the y-axis, which is option B. Approach 2 The circle is tangent to the y-axis, x = 0, if and only if the pair of simultaneous equations x2+ax+y2+by+c = 0 and x = 0 has a repeated root. Substituting x = 0 into the circle equation gives y2+by+c = 0, which has discriminant b2−4c. So there is a repeated root if and only if b2−4c = 0, which is equivalent to option B.

questions.text: p and q are real numbers, and the equation x|x| = px+q has exactly k distinct real solutions for x. Which one of the following is the complete list of possible values for k?
questions.workedSolution: Approach 1: graph sketching We are looking for the intersections of the graphs of y = x|x| and y = px+q. The latter is just a straight line graph, while the former is y = x2 when x ≥ 0 and y = −x2 when x < 0: y x This graph looks quite similar to the graph of y = x3: it has a point of inflexion at the origin. Any straight line will meet this graph at least once: if q > 0, then it will meet it for some x > 0, and if q < 0, then it will meet it for some x < 0. A straight line can meet the graph twice if it is tangent to the curve somewhere, and it can meet it three times, for example y = x. But a straight line cannot meet the curve four times. It can meet the curve at most twice for x ≥ 0, and at most twice for x < 0 (as each half of the curve is a quadratic, and a straight line and quadratic can meet at most twice). But if a straight line meets the quadratic twice in the region x < 0, then it must have q > 0, for example: y x It can then meet the curve only once in the region x > 0. The same applies if it meets the curve twice in the region x > 0. And if the line passes through the origin, then it can meet the curve in at most two other locations. So the correct answer is option E. Approach 2: algebraic

questions.text: Consider the following functions defined for x > 1: √ f(x) = log log x 2 2 g(x) = log log x 2 2 Which one of the following is true for all values of x > 1?
questions.workedSolution: The expressions for f(x) and g(x) look quite complicated, so we start by simplifying them using the laws of logarithms: √ f(x) = log log x 2 2 = log (1 log x) 2 2 2 = −1+log (log x) 2 2 g(x) = log log x 2 2 = 1 log (log x) 2 2 2 We now see that both f(x) and g(x) can be written as a function of log (log x). As x > 1, 2 2 log x > 0 and log (log x) can take any real value. 2 2 2 Let us write z = log (log x), so that we have f(x) = z −1 and g(x) = 1z. We can sketch a 2 2 2 graph showing these two functions of z: f(x) = z−1 g(x) = 1z 1 2 0 2 z We can calculate the intersection of y = z−1 and y = 1z, obtaining z = 2, y = 1. We can then see from the graph that f(x) ≥ g(x) when both are at least 1, and f(x) ≤ g(x) when both are at most 1, so either f(x) ≥ g(x) ≥ 1 or f(x) ≤ g(x) ≤ 1 (or both), which is option F. (Note that the ‘or’ in the options is inclusive ‘or’, so we are allowed to have both of the inequal- ities being true.)

questions.text: A student chooses two distinct real numbers x and y with 0 < x < y < 1. The student then attempts to draw a triangle ABC with: AB = 1 sinA = x sinB = y Which of the following statements is/are correct? I For some choice of x and y, there is exactly one triangle the student could draw. II For some choice of x and y, there are exactly two different triangles the student could draw. III For some choice of x and y, there are exactly three different triangles the student could draw. (Note that congruent triangles are considered to be the same.)
questions.workedSolution: Since we are only given a condition on sinA, the angle A could be acute or obtuse, and similarly for angle B. Let us write θ = sin−1x and ϕ = sin−1y for the acute angles; the respective obtuse angles are then 180°−θ and 180°−ϕ. As we are given x < y, we have θ < ϕ. We can now consider the four possible cases. • Case 1: both A and B are acute. Then we have this situation: ϕ θ A 1 B We therefore know the three angles of the triangle ABC (as given two, we automati- cally know the third) and one side, and this determines the triangle uniquely (regarding congruent triangles as the same). So we always obtain exactly one triangle from this case. • Case 2: A is obtuse and B is acute. Then A = 180°−θ and we have this situation: ϕ θ A 1 B Since θ < ϕ, the two lines (rays) from A and B shown in this diagram will never meet. Alternatively, we could note that A+B = 180° −θ+ϕ, and since ϕ−θ > 0, this means that the angles A and B sum to more than 180°. Therefore this case can never produce a triangle. • Case 3: A is acute and B is obtuse. Then B = 180°−ϕ and we have this situation: ϕ θ A 1 B Since θ < ϕ, the two lines (rays) from A and B shown will eventually meet, and so we get a unique triangle. Alternatively, we have A+B = θ+180°−ϕ = 180°−(ϕ−θ), and since ϕ−θ > 0, this means that the angles A and B sum to less than 180°, so they can be two angles of a triangle. So we always obtain exactly one triangle from this case.
questions.topicDisplay: Geometry, Trigonometry, Inequalities, Logic & Reasoning, Combinatorics

questions.text: The angle θ can take any of the values 1°, 2°, 3°, ..., 359°, 360°. For how many of these values of θ is it true that √ √ √ √ sinθ 1+sinθ 1−sinθ+cosθ 1+cosθ 1−cosθ = 0
questions.workedSolution: We can rewrite the given equation as sinθ (1+sinθ)(1−sinθ)+cosθ (1+cosθ)(1−cosθ) = 0 and then multiply out the brackets to give sinθ 1−sin2θ+cosθ 1−cos2θ = 0 which simplifies to √ sinθ cos2θ+cosθ sin2θ = 0. √ Itwouldbeeasyatthispointtomakethemistakeofsimplifying cos2θ tocosθ, butthiswould only work in the case that cosθ ≥ 0 as square roots are always positive. We can, though, write √ |cosθ| for this square root: we always have x2 = |x|. Therefore we can rewrite the equation as sinθ|cosθ|+cosθ|sinθ| = 0. If both sinθ and cosθ are positive, then this becomes 2sinθcosθ = 0, which cannot be true. Likewise, ifbothsinθ andcosθ arenegative, thisbecomes−2sinθcosθ = 0, whichagaincannot be true. However, if one is positive and one is negative, say sinθ > 0 and cosθ < 0, this becomes −sinθcosθ+sinθcosθ = 0, which is certainly true. The same holds if sinθ < 0 and cosθ > 0. Finally, if either sinθ = 0 or cosθ = 0, the equation is also satisfied. We therefore need to count the number of given angles for which sinθ and cosθ have opposite signs or one of them is zero. A quick sketch helps here: y x 0 90 180 270 360 −1 We see that the relevant ranges are 90° ≤ θ ≤ 180° and 270° ≤ θ ≤ 360°; x = 0° would also work, but is not one of the angles in the given list. There are 91 angles in each of the two ranges (remembering to include both endpoints), so there are 182 angles in total, which is option F.

questions.text: A sequence of functions f , f , f , ... is defined by 1 2 3 f (x) = |x| 1 f (x) = |f (x)+x| for n ≥ 1 n+1 n Find the value of 1 f (x)dx 99 −1
questions.workedSolution: It is helpful to get a feeling for what this sequence of functions looks like, so we will sketch the first few. We are only interested in the range from −1 to 1 (because that is what the integral asks for). The first function, f (x) = |x| is standard: y y = f (x) x −1 0 1 Note that when x < 0, this is the graph of y = −x, and when x ≥ 0, this is the graph of y = x. We next have f (x) = |f (x)+x|. We can calculate this on the two regions x < 0 and x ≥ 0 2 1 separately: whenx < 0,wegetf (x) = |−x+x| = 0,andwhenx > 0,wegetf (x) = |x+x| = 2x, 2 2 giving this graph: y y = f (x) x −1 0 1 We can determine f (x) and f (x) in a similar way: 3 4

questions.text: How many real solutions are there to the equation 2 cos4 θ – 5 cos2 θ + 3 = 0 in the interval 0 ≤ θ ≤ 2π ?
questions.workedSolution: This equation is a quadratic in cos2θ, and it factorises as (2cos2θ−3)(cos2θ−1) = 0. Therefore 2cos4θ−5cos2θ+3 = 0 if and only if cos2θ = 3 or cos2θ = 1. The first equation has no real solutions, so the original equation holds if and only if cos2θ = 1, or cosθ = ±1. cosθ = 1 has the solutions θ = 0 and θ = 2π in the interval 0 ≤ θ ≤ 2π, and cosθ = −1 has the solution θ = π in this interval. Therefore the original equation has three real solutions in the interval, and the correct option is C.

questions.text: Find the complete set of values of p for which the equation x 2 – 2 p x + y 2 – 6 y – p 2 + 8 p + 9 = 0 describes a circle in the x y-plane.
questions.workedSolution: We rewrite the equation in completed square form: starting from x2−2px+y2−6y−p2+8p+9 = 0, completing the square for x and y gives (x−p)2−p2+(y−3)2−9−p2+8p+9 = 0, which simplifies to (x−p)2+(y−3)2−2p2+8p = 0 or (x−p)2+(y−3)2 = 2p2−8p. This is a circle with squared radius 2p2−8p, as long as this is positive. So the complete set of values of p for which the original equation describes a circle in the xy-plane are those for which 2p2−8p > 0, or p2−4p > 0. This factorises as p(p−4) > 0, which has roots p = 0, p = 4, so the complete set of values in which the inequality holds is p < 0 or p > 4, which is option D.

questions.text: Given the following statements about a function f • f Ǝ (x) = a for all x • f (0) = 1, f (1) = 2 • 1 0 f ( )d x x ³ = 1 find the value of a.
questions.workedSolution: Using the first statement, f(x) = a for all x, we can integrate to get f(x) = ax+b for some constant b, and a further integration gives f(x) = 1ax2+bx+c for some constant c. The second statement then yields f(0) = c = 1 and f(1) = 1a+b+1 = 2, so a+2b = 2. Finally, we can now use the third statement: f(x)dx = 1ax3+ 1bx2+x 1 6 2 0 = 1a+ 1b+1 6 2 = 1 so that a+3b = 0. Combining the simultaneous equations a+2b = 2 a+3b = 0 gives b = −2 and a = 6, so the correct answer is option F.

questions.text: r r + 3 These sectors of circles are similar. The arc length of the smaller sector is 6. The difference between the areas of the sectors is 21. Find the positive difference between the perimeters of the sectors.
questions.workedSolution: Suppose that the angle of the sector is θ (in radians). Then the arc length of the smaller sector is rθ and the area is 1r2θ. For the smaller sector, this gives rθ = 6, so θ = 6/r. Therefore the area is 1r2(6/r) = 3r. Likewise,sincethelargersectorhasthesameangleasthesmallerone,itsarclengthis(r+3)(6/r) and its area is 1(r+3)2(6/r) = 3(r+3)2/r. We are told the difference between the areas, so we have 3(r+3)2 −3r = 21. r Multiplying this by r and rearranging gives 3(r+3)2−3r2−21r = 0 which we can expand and simplify to give −3r+27 = 0, so r = 9. We can now calculate the arc length of the larger sector; it is (r+3)(6/r) = 8. Therefore, the perimeter of the first sector is 2r+6 = 24 and the perimeter of the second sector is 2(r+3)+8 = 32. The positive difference is 8, which is option C.

questions.text: The terms x n of a sequence follow the rule 1 nx + = + + n n x p x q where p and q are real numbers. Given that x 1 = 3, x 2 = 5, and x 3 = 7, find the value of x 4
questions.workedSolution: We write out the formulae for x , x and x in terms of p and q, using the values x = 3, x = 5 2 3 4 1 2 and x = 7: 3+p n = 1 : 5 = 3+q 5+p n = 2 : 7 = 5+q 7+p n = 3 : x = 7+q Multiplying the first equation by 3+q and the second by 5+q gives 15+5q = 3+p 35+7q = 5+p These are simultaneous equations for p and q; subtracting gives 20 + 2q = 2 so q = −9 and therefore p = −33. Substituting these into the expression for x gives 7−33 x = = 13 7−9 so the answer is option H.

questions.text: Given that 2 2 log 20 log 5 d x x ³ = 2 log M what is the value of M ?
questions.workedSolution: We calculate the integral and use the laws of logarithms; we obtain log 20 xdx = 1x2log 2 log 5 log 5 2 = 1(log 20)2− 1(log 5)2 2 2 2 2 = 1 (log 20)2−(log 5)2 2 2 2 = 1 (log 4+log 5)2−(log 5)2 2 2 2 2 = 1 (2+log 5)2−(log 5)2 2 2 2 = 1 4+4log 5+(log 5)2−(log 5)2 2 2 2 2 = 1(4+4log 5) 2 2 = 2+2log 5 = log 4+log 25 2 2 = log 100 so the answer is option F.

questions.text: Find the finite area enclosed between the line y = 0 and the curve y = x 2 – 4| x | – 12
questions.workedSolution: The line y = 0 is the x-axis; the curve y = x2 −4|x|−12 is y = x2 −4x−12 when x ≥ 0 and y = x2+4x−12 when x < 0, so we sketch these two graphs. The curve y = x2 −4x−12 = (x−6)(x+2) intersects the x-axis at x = 6 and x = −2 and has a minimum half way between them, at x = 2. Since we are only considering x ≥ 0, we only observe one point of intersection with the x-axis. Similarly, the curve y = x2+4x−12 = (x+6)(x−2) intersects the x-axis at x = −6 and x = 2 and has a minimum half way between them, at x = −2. Since we are only considering x < 0, we only observe one point of intersection with the x-axis. We can now sketch the graph, where we have shown the parts of the quadratics we are not using as dashed lines: −6 −2 2 6 The finite area enclosed between the x-axis and the graph y = x2−4|x|−12 is therefore between x = −6 and x = 6. We could calculate the integrals of the two quadratics, one between x = −6 and x = 0 and the other between x = 0 and x = 6. But we could also notice that the graph is symmetrical about the y-axis, so we only need to find one of these and double the answer. We have 6 x3 6 x2−4x−12dx = −2x2−12x 0 0 = −2×62−12×6−0 = −72 so 6 x2−4|x|−12dx = −144 and the (unsigned) area required is 144, which is option E. −6

questions.text: A geometric sequence has first term a and common ratio r, where a and r are positive integers and r is greater than 1. The sum of the first n terms of this sequence is denoted by Sn It is given that the terms of the sequence satisfy S30 – S20 = k S10 for some positive integer k. What is the smallest possible value of k ?
questions.workedSolution: a(rn−1) We use the formula S = and substitute into the given equation: n r−1 a(r30−1) a(r20−1) a(r10−1) − = k . r−1 r−1 r−1 We can multiply this by r−1 and divide by a to get (r30−1)−(r20−1) = k(r10−1), which simplifies to r30−r20 = k(r10−1). Factorising the left hand side then gives r20(r10−1) = k(r10−1); as r > 1, we can divide by r10−1 = 0 to obtain r20 = k. Therefore k is a 20th power, and the smallest possible value is 220, which is option B.

questions.text: This question is about pairs of functions f and g that satisfy f (x) – g (x) = 2 sin x f (x) g (x) = cos2 x for all real numbers x . Across all solutions for f (x), what is the minimum value that f (x) attains for any x ?
questions.workedSolution: It may well require reading this question a couple of times to understand what it is asking! It seems like we need to find all possible functions f(x) and g(x), then for each one, to find the minimum value that f(x) takes, and then to find the minimum of all the minima. There might, of course, be some shortcuts! Let us try to solve the equations to find f(x). The first equation rearranges to give g(x) = f(x)−2sinx, so we can substitute this into the second equation to give f(x)(f(x)−2sinx) = cos2x. This is a quadratic in f(x), so we can rearrange it into the standard form and use the quadratic formula: (f(x))2−(2sinx)f(x)−cos2x = 0, hence 2sinx± 4sin2x+4cos2x f(x) = = sinx±1. So there are two possible functions f(x), namely f(x) = sinx + 1 and f(x) = sinx − 1. The minimum value taken by these for any x is 0 for the first function and −2 for the second, so the minimum possible value is −2, which is option E.

questions.text: A sequence of translations is applied to the graph of y = x 3 Which of the following graphs could be the result of this sequence of translations? I y = x 3 – 3 x 2 + 9 x – 27 II y = x 3 – 9 x 2 + 27 x – 3 III y = 27 x 3 – 9 x 2 + x – 3
questions.workedSolution: Any sequence of translations is equivalent to a translation in the x-direction followed by a translation in the y-direction (where the translations could be zero). Translating y = x3 by a in the x-direction gives the graph y = (x−a)3 = x3−3ax2+3a2x−a3. Then translating by b in the y-direction gives the graph y = x3−3ax2+3a2x−a3+b. Now considering the three graphs: I The x2 term is −3x2, which means we require a = 1. Substituting this into the above general equation gives y = x3−3x2+3x−1+b, which does not match the given equation. So this is not a translation of y = x3. II The x2 term is −9x2, so we require a = 3. Substituting gives y = x3−9x2+27x−27+b, which matches the equation with b = 24, so this is a translation of y = x3. III The x3 term does not match here, so this is not a translation of y = x3. Therefore the answer is option C, II only.

questions.text: Evaluate ( ) 100 1– 10 1 log 3 n n=¦
questions.workedSolution: We have log (31−n) = (1−n)log 3, so the sum becomes 10 10 100 100 log (31−n) = (1−n)log 3 10 10 n=1 n=1 = (log 3) (1−n). n=1 Now the sum is actually an arithmetic series: expanding it gives (1−n) = 0+(−1)+(−2)+···+(−99) n=1 = −(0+1+2+···+99) = −1 ×100(0+99×1) = −4950 using the formula S = 1n(2a+(n−1)d). (Alternatively, we could have used the formula for n 2 the nth triangular number.) Therefore the original sum evaluates to −4950log 3, which is option A.

questions.text: A family of quadratic curves is given by yk = 2 2 2 – + + 4 + 3 2 2 k k x k § · ¨ ¸ © ¹ where k is any real number and yk is a function of x. All these curves are sketched, and the point with the lowest y-coordinate among all the curves yk is (a, b). Find the value of a + b
questions.workedSolution: Theformulafory isgivenincompletedsquareform. Theminimumofthegraphy = a(x−b)2+c, k where a > 0, is at x = b, and the minimum value is c. Therefore the minimum of y is at k (k, k2 +4k+3). 2 2 The minimum of the minimum y-coordinates is therefore the minimum of k2 +4k+3. We can find this minimum either by completing the square or by differentiating this with respect to k. Let us use the latter method here as it seems simpler. We have d k2 +4k+3 = k+4 dk 2 so the minimum occurs at k = −4, giving k k2 , +4k+3 = (−2,−5) 2 2 so that a+b = −7, which is option D.

questions.text: Given that 3 3 3 3 2 2 + – a b b a § ·§ · ¨ ¸¨ ¸ © ¹© ¹ = 2 where a and b are real numbers, what is the least value of a b ?
questions.workedSolution: Expanding the brackets gives 4 √ 2−a3b3+ −2 = 2 a3b3 so 4 √ −a3b3 = 2. a3b3 We are interested in the least possible value of ab, so we can find the least possible value of a3b3 √ and take the cube root. Let us simplify the notation by writing x = a3b3, so 4 −x = 2. x √ √ Multiplying by x gives a quadratic: 4−x2 = 2x, which we can rearrange to x2+ 2x−4 = 0 and then solve using the quadratic formula: √ √ √ √ − 2± 2+16 − 2±3 2 √ √ x = = = −2 2 or 2. 2 2 √ Since we want the least possible value of x, we tak √ e x = a3b3 = −2 2 = −2 3 2, and so the 3 1 1 minimum possible value of ab is (−22)3 = −22 = − 2, which is option A.

questions.text: A circle has centre O and radius 6. P, Q and R are points on the circumference with angle POQ  π 2 The area of the triangle POQ is 9 3 What is the greatest possible area of triangle PRQ ?
questions.workedSolution: Let us start by drawing the triangle POQ in the circle: O 6 6 2θ P Q (We have called the angle 2θ as we will shortly want to consider half of the angle.) √ We are told that the area of POQ is 9 3, so we can use the formula for the area of a triangle giventwosidesandtheincludedangletodetermine2θ: theareaformulaisA = 1absinC, which in this case gives √ 1 ×6×6×sin2θ = 9 3 √ so sin2θ = 3. Since 2θ ≥ π, we must have 2θ = 2π (or 120°). 2 2 3 We can now work out the length PQ by dropping a perpendicular from O to PQ: O 6 6 θ θ P Q S √ √ We have PS = SQ = 6sinθ = 6sin 1π = 3 3, so PQ = 6 3, and we also have OS = 6cosθ = 6cos 1π = 3. We can now work out the maximum possible area of the triangle PQR. The triangle PQR looks like this: R O P √ Q 6 3 Fixing PQ and allowing R to move, we see that the largest possible area of PQR occurs when R is as far as possible from PQ, as the area of the triangle is 1 ×base×height. When R is at the top of the circle (in our diagram), we have this configuration:

questions.text: A rectangle is drawn in the region enclosed by the curves p and q , where p (x) = 8 – 2 x 2 q (x) = x 2 – 2 such that the sides of the rectangle are parallel to the x- and y-axes. What is the maximum possible area of the rectangle?
questions.workedSolution: We start by sketching the two curves to understand what is happening. They are both quadratics. The graph of p has a maximum at (0,8) and the graph of q has a minimum at (0,−2). They are both symmetrical about x = 0 (the y-axis). It might be helpful to work out where they intersect: 8−2x2 = x2 −2 implies 3x2 = 10, so x = 10/3, which is between 1.5 and 2. The graph of these therefore looks like this, where we have also sketched a possible rectangle. For the rectangle to have the maximum possible area, it must meet the graphs at all four of its vertices. Let us suppose that the vertices of the rectangle are at x = ±a, with 0 < a < 10/3. Then the width of the rectangle is 2a and the height is (8−2a2)−(a2−2) = 10−3a2. Therefore the area is A = 2a(10−3a2) = 20a−6a3. To maximise this area, we differentiate with respect to a to find the stationary points. We have dA = 20−18a2 da √ so there is a stationary point when this is zero, at a = ± 20/18 = ± 10/9 = ±1 10. We are only interested in the positive value, and this is less than 10/3, so it is a valid value for our problem. We therefore obtain the maximum possible area: it is A = 2a(10−3a2) √ = 2 10(10− 1 ×10) 3√ 3 = 2 10× 2 ×10 3 √ 3 40 10 = which is option H.

questions.text: The solutions to 7 x 4 – 6 x 2 + 1 = 0 are - cos θ and - cos ȕ. Which one of the following equations has solutions - sin θ and - sin ȕ ?
questions.workedSolution: The given quartic can be thought of as a quadratic in x2, and as such it has roots √ √ 6± 62−28 3± 2 x2 = = . 14 7 Since the roots of the quartic are ±cosθ and ±cosβ, these x2 values are the values of cos2θ and cos2β (in some order). Now sin2θ = 1−cos2θ and sin2β = 1−cos2β, so √ √ 3± 2 4∓ 2 sin2θ,sin2β = 1− = . 7 7 √ √ Therefore the equations x2 − 4+ 2 = 0 and x2 − 4− 2 = 0 have solutions ±sinθ and ±sinβ 7 7 (again, in some order, and x is not the same as earlier). It follows that the product of these two equations will have the four solutions ±sinθ and ±sinβ; this equation is √ √ 4+ 2 4− 2 x2− x2− = 0. 7 7 To obtain the form given in the question, we first expand the brackets: √ √ √ √ 4+ 2 4− 2 4+ 2 4− 2 x4− + x2+ · = 0 7 7 7 7 then perform the surd calculations to give 8 2 x4− x2+ = 0. 7 7 Finally, multiplying by 7 gives 7x2−8x+2 = 0, which is option B.

questions.text: 30° x – 1 – x 2 + 6 x – 5 Find the complete set of values of x for which there are two non-congruent triangles with the side lengths and angle as shown in the diagram.
questions.workedSolution: We first require that the lengths shown are both positive; x−1 > 0 if and only if x > 1, and −x2+6x−5 = −(x−5)(x−1) > 0 if and only if 1 < x < 5. Therefore we require 1 < x < 5 to ensure both lengths are positive. There are two non-congruent triangles when the side marked x−1 can be drawn in two config- urations: x−1 −x2+6x−5 30° This is possible when the following conditions are satisfied: • x−1 < −x2+6x−5 (otherwise one of the possibilities will lie to the right of the 30° angle in the above diagram) • x−1 > (−x2+6x−5)sin30° (so that the distance from the end of the −x2+6x−5 side to the base is at least x−1, making it possible to form a triangle; if there is equality here, then the dashed circle will be tangent to the base of the triangle, and there will only be one possibility) The first inequality rearranges as x2−5x+4 < 0, which factorises as (x−1)(x−4) < 0, giving 1 < x < 4. The second equality, using sin30° = 1, becomes 2x−2 > −x2+6x−5, so x2−4x+3 > 0. This factorises as (x−1)(x−3) > 0, so x < 1 or x > 3. Requiring both of these inequalities to hold thus gives 3 < x < 4; this also satifies the positivity requirement we started with (1 < x < 5), and so the answer is option D.

questions.text: It is given that f (x) = x 2(x – 1)2 (x – 2) g (x) = – p (x – q)2 (x – r)2 where p, q and r are positive and q < r Find the set of values of q and r that guarantees the greatest number of distinct real solutions of the equation f (x) = g (x) for all p.
questions.workedSolution: The equation f(x) = g(x) is a polynomial equation with degree 5, so has at most 5 distinct real solutions; we wish there to be 5 such solutions for all values of p. We would have fewer than 5 distinct real solutions if we either have a square factor of the form (x−a)2 for some a, or if the graph of y = f(x)−g(x) has fewer than four turning (stationary) points, or if this graph has a minimum point above the x-axis or a maximum point below it, for then it would not cross the x-axis 5 times. Let us write h(x) = f(x)−g(x) = x2(x−1)2(x−2)+p(x−q)2(x−r)2 We can try expanding and differentiating this, but that looks horrible. Let us think about the above description, though, remembering that there need to be 5 distinct real solutions for all values of p. The graph of y = f(x) = x2(x−1)2(x−2) intersects the x-axis at x = 0 (where it is tangent to the axis, because it is a double root), x = 1 (likewise) and x = 2, and it is positive when x > 2 and negative or zero when x < 2, so the graph looks like this: 1 2 The graph of y = g(x) = −p(x−q)2(x−r)2 has two points of intersection with the x-axis, one at x = q and one at x = r, and at both of these points, the graph is tangent to the axis. (In the case q = r, there is just one point of tangency.) The graph is entirely below or touching the x-axis as we are given that p > 0. It looks like this: q r If r > 2, then the graph of y = g(x) will entirely miss one of the “valleys” of y = f(x) if p is large, as it will if r < 1; in this case, there would be at most three points of intersection for

questions.text: Circle C1 is defined as x 2 + y 2 = 25 A second circle C2 has radius 4 and centre (a, b) where – 2 ≤ a ≤ 2 and – 3 ≤ b ≤ 3 If the centre of C2 is equally likely to be located anywhere within the given range, what is the probability that C2 intersects C1 ?
questions.workedSolution: Note first that the region where the centre of C may lie is entirely within the circle C , as 2 1 22+32 = 13 < 25. As the radius of C is 5, the circle C will intersect C if and only if the centre of C is at least 1 2 1 2 a distance of 1 from the origin (the centre of C ). This is because in this case, a line from the centre of C passing through the centre of C will reach the circle C before (or as) it reaches 1 2 1 the circle C , but a line from the centre of C in the opposite direction will reach C first. If, 2 1 2 though, the centre of C is at a distance of less than 1 from the origin, the furthest point on C 2 2 from the origin will be at a distance of less than 1+4 = 5 from the origin, so C lies entirely inside C . The area of the allowed (a,b) region is 4×6 = 24. The (a,b) region where the circles do not intersect is a circle of radius 1, with area π, lying entirely within the allowed region, so the region where the circles do intersect has area 24−π. 24−π Thus the probability of intersection is , which is option F.

questions.text: n is the number of points of intersection of the graphs y = | x 2 – a 2 | and y = a 2 | x – 1| where a is a real number. What is the smallest value of n that is not possible?
questions.workedSolution: The graph y = |x2 −a2| intersects the x-axis at ±a. The graph y = a2|x−1| intersects the x-axis at x = 1. Replacing a with −a does not change the equations, so we may as well assume that a ≥ 0. Here is a sketch of the graphs with a = 2; we note that the graphs always intersect at x = 0 (and y = a2 there): −2 1 2 Weimmediatelyseethatwhena > 1,therewillbe4pointsofintersection: thegraphy = a2|x−1| isbelowthe“hill”at(1,0),sointersectsittwiceasxmovesawayfromx = 1,andthenintersects the quadratic y = x2−a2 for some x < −a and some x > a as the quadratic grows faster than the linear y = −a2(x−1) or y = a2(x−1) in these regions. What happens when a = 1? In this case, the equations become y = |x2−1| and y = x−1. The derivative of the quadratic y = x2−1 is 2x, so the gradient is just over 2 when x is just over 1. (There is no gradient at the point x = 1.) Therefore the graph looks like this: −1 1 Weseethatwehavelostonepointofintersection: therearenowonlythreepointsofintersection. What happens if 0 < a < 1? Now the vertex of y = a2|x−1| will be to the right of x = a, where the quadratic meets the axis, and so there will be no points of intersection there – the quadratic grows too quickly to meet the linear function for x > 1. (To be more precise: the gradient of the quadratic to the right of x = a is 2x > 2a > a2, so the quadratic will be growing faster than the linear function.) On the left of x = a, we have one point of intersection to the left of x = −a as before. There is also a point of intersection at (0,a2). Since this is the vertex of the quadratic, the linear

questions.text: Determine the number of stationary points on the curve with equation y = 3 x 4 + 4 x 3 + 6 x 2 – 5
questions.workedSolution: We differentiate to obtain dy = 12x3+12x2+12x. dx We solve the equation dy = 0 to find the number of stationary points: dx 12x3+12x2+12x = 0 if and only if 12x(x2+x+1) = 0. So either x = 0 or x2+x+1 = 0. But this quadratic has discriminant 12−4×1 = −3 < 0, so it has no real solutions. Thus there is only one stationary point, at x = 0, and the answer is option B.

questions.text: Find the coefficient of the x 5 term in the expansion of ( ) 5 5 =0 1+ × i i x x ¦
questions.workedSolution: We expand the brackets in the first term using the binomial theorem and write out the sum in full to get (1+5x+10x2+10x3+5x4+x5)×(1+x+x2+x3+x4+x5). The x5 term in the product is obtained by taking all possible pairs of a term in the first bracket and a term in the second whose product is cx5 for some number c, and then adding these together. Therefore the x5 term in the product is 1·x5+5x·x4+10x2·x3+10x3·x2+5x4·x+x5·1 = (1+5+10+10+5+1)x5 = 32x5. Therefore the correct answer is option E.

questions.text: Consider the following statement about the positive integer n if n is prime, then n 2 + 2 is not prime Which of the following is a counterexample to this statement? I n = 2 II n = 3 III n = 4
questions.workedSolution: I n = 2 is prime and n2+2 = 6 is not prime, so this case satisfies the statement and is not a counterexample. II n = 3 is prime but n2 +2 = 11 is prime, so this is a counterexample as it does not satisfy the statement. III n = 4 is not prime, so it satisfies the statement and is not a counterexample. Therefore only II provides a counterexample, which is option C.

questions.text: The point P has coordinates ( p, q ), and the equation of a circle is x 2 + 2 f x + y 2 + 2 g y + h = 0 where f, g, h, p and q are all real constants. Let L be the distance between the centre of the circle and the point P. Which one of the following is sufficient on its own to be able to calculate L ?

options.A: the values of f, g and h
options.B: the values of f, g, p and q
options.C: the values of f, h, p and q
options.D: the values of g, h, p and q
options.E: none of the options A-D is sufficient on its own

questions.workedSolution: We can rewrite the equation of circle by completing the square to find its centre. We get: (x+f)2−f2+(y+g)2−g2+h = 0 which we can rearrange as (x+f)2+(y+g)2 = f2+g2−h. Therefore the centre is at (−f,−g) (and the radius is f2+g2−h, but that is not relevant to us here). Then the distance between the centre of the circle and the point P is given by L2 = (p+f)2+(q+g)2 so we need the values of f, g, p and q. Hence the correct answer is option B.

questions.text: A straight line L passes through (1, 2). Let P be the statement if the y-intercept of L is negative, then the x-intercept of L is positive. Which of the following statements must be true? I P II the converse of P III the contrapositive of P
questions.workedSolution: I Let us sketch a line through (1,2) with a negative y-intercept: y x It is clear from this sketch that if the line has a negative y-intercept, it must have a positive x-intercept. (A more formal proof goes as follows: since y < 0 when x = 0 and y > 0 when x = 1, theremustbesomevalueofxwith0 < x < 1wherey = 0. Thereforethex-intercept must lie between 0 and 1.) II The converse of P reads: If the x-intercept of L is positive, then the y-intercept of L is negative. This is the case if the x-intercept is between 0 and 1, as in the previous sketch, but if the x-intercept is greater than 1, this is no longer true: y x So the converse of P is false. III The contrapositive of P has the same truth value as P itself, so the contrapositive of P is true. Therefore only I and III are true, and the correct answer is option F.

questions.text: A list consists of n integers. Consider the following statements: P : n is odd. Q : The median of the list is one of the numbers in the list. Which one of the following is true?

options.A: P is necessary and sufficient for Q.
options.B: P is necessary but not sufficient for Q.
options.C: P is sufficient but not necessary for Q.
options.D: P is not necessary and not sufficient for Q.

questions.workedSolution: Suppose P is true. Since there are an odd number of integers in the list, there is a unique middle one (when they are written in increasing order), so this is the median. Therefore Q is true, and P is sufficient for Q. Now suppose that Q is true. It does not follow that n is odd; here is a counterexample: if the list is 2, 2, then n = 2 and the median is 2, so Q is true, but P is false. (In general, any list with n even and the middle pair of numbers equal is a counterexample.) Therefore P is not necessary for Q. The correct answer is option C.

questions.text: Consider the following claim: The difference between two consecutive positive cube numbers is always prime. Here is an attempted proof of this claim: I ( x + 1)3 = x 3 + 3x 2 + 3x + 1 II Taking x to be a positive integer, the difference between two consecutive cube numbers can be expressed as ( x + 1)3 – x 3 = 3x 2 + 3x + 1 III It is impossible to factorise 3x 2 + 3x + 1 into two linear factors with integer coefficients because its discriminant is negative. IV Therefore for every positive integer value of x the integer 3x 2 + 3x + 1 cannot be factorised. V Hence, the difference between two consecutive cube numbers will always be prime. Which of the following best describes this proof ?

options.A: The proof is completely correct, and the claim is true.
options.B: The proof is completely correct, but there are counterexamples to the claim.
options.C: The proof is wrong, and the first error occurs on line I.
options.D: The proof is wrong, and the first error occurs on line II.
options.E: The proof is wrong, and the first error occurs on line III.
options.F: The proof is wrong, and the first error occurs on line IV.
options.G: The proof is wrong, and the first error occurs on line V.

questions.workedSolution: Searching for small counterexamples to the claim does not find any; the first few differences are all prime. So we do have to check the proof in detail, as it might be correct. The algebraic expansion on line I is correct. The expression in line II is also correct, using the expansion from line I. For line III, if we could write 3x2+3x+1 = (ax+b)(cx+d) with real coefficients a, b, c and d, then we would have real roots of the equation 3x3 +3x+1 = 0, namely x = −b and x = −d. a c But the discriminant is 32−4×3×1 = −3 < 0, so this is impossible. So this statement is true. Line IV is more tricky: just because the algebraic expression is not factorisable into algebraic factorsdoesnot meanthattheintegeritrepresentsforanyparticularvalueofxisnotfactorisable in integers. As a simpler example, the algebraic expression x2 +1 is not factorisable into real algebraic factors, yet substituting integers for x shows that the integer value can sometimes be factorised: taking x = 3 gives 32+1 = 10 = 2×5, for example. So this line of the argument is not valid. Line V does follow from lines II and IV. Therefore the proof is wrong, and the first (and only) error occurs on line IV (option F). It is worth noting that the ‘opposite’ argument does work: if an algebraic expression does factorise as (ax+b)(cx+d) with a, b, c and d integers, then the value of original expression always factorises (as an integer) when x is an integer, just by substituting the value of x into the factorised form. (It is possible, though, that one of the factors is 1, so the integer may be prime for some value(s) of x.) It also turns out that in this case, if we search far enough, we do find a counterexample to the claim: 63−53 = 216−125 = 91 = 7×13. (It might have been the case that the claim was true even though the attempted proof had an error.)

questions.text: A selection, S, of n terms is taken from the arithmetic sequence 1, 4, 7, 10, … , 70. Consider the following statement: ( ) * There are two distinct terms in S whose sum is 74. What is the smallest value of n for which ( ) * is necessarily true?
questions.workedSolution: We first determine the number of terms in the whole sequence: there are 70−1+1 = 23+1 = 24 terms. We next determine the number of pairs that sum to 74. The pairs are: 4+70 7+67 . . . 34+40 [37+37] The final pair is excluded because the two terms have to be distinct to fit the rule in (*). The number of valid pairs is therefore 34−4 +1 = 10+1 = 11. If a selection S does not satisfy the rule in (*), then it can have at most one term from each of these pairs, so it has to leave out at least 11 terms. So it can have at most 24−11 = 13 terms. Therefore if S has at least 14 terms, it must satisfy (*). We also need to show that if S only has 13 terms, it might not satisfy (*). We can include 1, 37 and one term from each listed pair, giving 2+11 = 13 terms. These 13 terms do not contain a distinct pair summing to 37. Hencethesmallestvalueofnwhichforces(*)tobetrueis14,andthecorrectanswerisoptionC..

questions.text: Consider the following statement: (*) For all real numbers x, if x < k then x 2 < k What is the complete set of values of k for which (*) is true?
questions.workedSolution: The statement (*) does not restrict us to positive real numbers x, so whatever the value of k, we can always find a large negative number x with x < k and x2 ≥ k. We can even be explicit about this (though that is not required to answer this question): if √ k ≤ 0, we can take x = k−1 in which case x2 > 0 ≥ k, and if k > 0, we can take x = − k so x < 0 < k and x2 = k ≥ k. Therefore (*) is true for no real values of k, and the correct option is A.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is/are true? I For all real numbers x and for all positive integers n, x < n II For all real numbers x, there exists a positive integer n such that x < n III There exists a real number x such that for all positive integers n, x < n
questions.workedSolution: I We can find a counterexample to this statement: taking x = 2 and n = 1 shows that this statement is false. II This is true; if x ≤ 0, we can take n = 1, and if x > 0, we can take n to be the smallest integer greater than x. III This is true; we can take x = 0. (If, though, we replaced ‘positive integers’ with ‘integers’, the resulting statement would be false.) Therefore II and III are true, and the correct option is G.

questions.text: S R O Q P x y x z The diagram shows a kite PQRS whose diagonals meet at O. OP = x OQ = y OR = x OS = z Which of the following is necessary and sufficient for angle SPQ to be a right angle?
questions.workedSolution: The angle SPQ is a right angle if and only if the triangle SPQ satisfies Pythagoras’s theorem, that is, if and only if SP2+PQ2 = SQ2. Since PQRS is a kite, its diagonals intersect at right angles, so both SOP and POQ are right- angled triangles. Therefore, again by Pythagoras, SP2 = x2+z2 PQ2 = x2+y2. Substituting this, along with SQ = y+z, into the above equation gives the following necessary and sufficient condition for angle SPQ to be a right angle: x2+z2+x2+y2 = (y+z)2. Expanding and simplifying, this is equivalent to 2x2 = 2yz or x2 = yz. Therefore option C gives a necessary and sufficient condition. (One should also check that none of the others are necessary and sufficient; we could take x = 2, y = 4, z = 1: this satisfies option C but none of the others.)

questions.text: Place the following integrals in order of size, starting with the smallest. P = 1 0 2 d x x ³ Q = 1 0 2 d x x ³ R = ( ) 1 0 2 d x x ³
questions.workedSolution: We do not know how to integrate any of these, so we will instead compare the integrands as the integrals are all from 0 to 1. √ We have 2 x = 2x/2, which makes R look a little simpler. Since x/2 < x for 0 < x < 1, 2x/2 < 2x in this interval, and so R < Q. √ √ Now in this interval, we also have x < x (as x2 < x), so 2x < 2 x, hence Q < P. Combining these, we find that R < Q < P, so the correct option is F.

questions.text: Consider the statement (*) about a real number x: (*) There exists a real number y such that x – x y + y is negative. For how many real values of x is (*) true?

options.D: all except exactly two values of x
options.E: all except exactly one value of x

questions.workedSolution: For each fixed value of x, we can think about this expression as being a function of y. We can write the expression as (1−x)y+x, which is just my+c where m = 1−x and c = x. This is the equation of a straight line graph, so it takes every real value, both positive and negative, as long as m = 0. Thecasem = 0occursexactlywhenx = 1,andinthiscase,theexpressionbecomes(1−1)y+1 = 1, so it is always 1, for every value of y. Therefore (*) is true for all except exactly one value of x, namely x = 1, and the correct answer is option E.

questions.text: Consider the two inequalities: +5 x < +11 x +11 x < +1 x Which one of the following is correct?

options.A: There is no real number for which both inequalities are true.
options.B: There is exactly one real number for which both inequalities are true.
options.C: The real numbers for which both inequalities are true form an interval of length 1.
options.D: The real numbers for which both inequalities are true form an interval of length 2.
options.E: The real numbers for which both inequalities are true form an interval of length 3.
options.F: The real numbers for which both inequalities are true form an interval of length 4.
options.G: The real numbers for which both inequalities are true form an interval of length 5.

questions.workedSolution: To solve this question, we use the fact that |x−a| can be understood as the distance of x from a. The first inequality is true if and only if x is closer to −5 than to −11, i.e., if and only if x > −8. The second inequality is true if and only if x is closer to −11 than to −1, i.e., if and only if x < −6. Therefore both inequalities are true if and only if −8 < x < −6, which is an interval of length 2, which is option D. One could also do this question by sketching the graphs of y = |x+5| and y = |x+11| and working out where they intersect, and so on. But that requires a lot more work than than the approach presented here.

questions.text: The real numbers x, y and z are all greater than 1, and satisfy the equations log x y = z and log y z = x Which one of the following equations for log z x must be true?
questions.workedSolution: Since log y = z, we have y = xz. Likewise z = yx. Combining these gives x z = yx = (xz)x = xxz. 1 1 Therefore raising the equation to the power of gives z1/xz = x, hence log x = , which is xz z xz option F.

questions.text: In this question, a1, …, a 100 and b1, …, b 100 and c1, …, c 100 are three sequences of integers such that a n ≤ b n + c n for each n. Which of the following statements must be true? I (minimum of a1, …, a 100 ) ≤ (minimum of b1, …, b 100 ) + (minimum of c1, …, c 100 ) II (minimum of a1, …, a 100 )  (minimum of b1, …, b 100 ) + (minimum of c1, …, c 100 ) III (maximum of a1, …, a 100 ) ≤ (maximum of b1, …, b 100 ) + (maximum of c1, …, c 100 )
questions.workedSolution: For each of these, we can try to construct a counterexample, and if that doesn’t work, we may understand why the statement must be true. For simplicity in the explanations below, let’s write A = minimum of a , ..., a min 1 100 B = minimum of b , ..., b min 1 100 C = minimum of c , ..., c min 1 100 and similarly for A etc. max I Let’s suppose that B = 0 and C = 0. Can we then make the minimum of the a s min min n greater than zero? Yes, we can: if we take b = 0 and the rest of the b s to be 100, and we take c = 0 and 1 n 100 the rest of the c s to be 100, then we can have a = 100 for every n, so the minimum of n n the a s is 100. n Therefore this statement is not necessarily true. II If we try the same example as in I, we find that A = 100, which is greater than B + min min C , so the inequality holds in this case. min But to get a counterexample, we want to make A small. Helpfully, the condition a ≤ min n b +c allows us to make each a as small as we like. So if we take a = 0, b = 1 and n n n n n c = 1 for all n, then the condition will be satisfied for each n, but we will have A = 0 n min and B = C = 1, so A < B +C in this case. min min min min min Therefore this statement is not necessarily true either. III Both of the counterexamples we used for I and II satisfy this statement. It is not obvious how to construct a counterexample: every simple example satisfies this statement. So let us instead try to prove it. Each a satisfies the condition a ≤ b +c . Now b ≤ B and c ≤ C for each n, n n n n n max n max so a ≤ B +C for each n. But this means that the maximum of all of the a s also n max max n satisfies this condition, that is A ≤ B +C , so the given statement must be true. max max max Therefore only statement III must be true, and the correct answer is option D.
questions.topicDisplay: Sequences & Series, Logic & Reasoning, Number Theory

questions.text: A student answered the following question: a and b are non-zero real numbers. Prove that the equation x 3 + a x 2 + b = 0 has three distinct real roots if 3 4 27 + 27 a b b § · ¨ ¸ © ¹ < 0 Here is the student’s solution: I We differentiate y = x 3 + a x 2 + b to get d d y x = 3x 2 + 2a x = x (3x + 2a) Solving d d y x = 0 shows that the stationary points are at (0, b) and 3 2 4 – , + 3 27 a a b § · ¨ ¸ © ¹ II If 3 4 27 + 27 a b b § · ¨ ¸ © ¹ < 0, then b and 3 4 + 27 a b must have opposite signs, and so one of the stationary points is above the x-axis and one is below. III If the cubic has three distinct real roots, then one of the stationary points is above the x-axis and one is below. IV Hence if 3 4 27 + 27 a b b § · ¨ ¸ © ¹ < 0, then the equation has three distinct real roots. Which one of the following options best describes the student’s solution?

options.A: It is a completely correct solution.
options.B: The student has instead proved the converse of the statement in the question.
options.C: The solution is wrong, because the student should have stated step II after step III.
options.D: The solution is wrong, because the student should have shown the converse of the result in step II.
options.E: The solution is wrong, because the student should have shown the converse of the result in step III.

questions.workedSolution: Consideringtheofferedoptions,thefocusisontheorderofthestepsandwhetherthestepsprove what they should be proving; there is no requirement for us to check the algebraic calculations themselves. 4a3 The task is to prove that if 27b b+ < 0, then x3 +ax2 +b = 0 has three distinct real roots. In step I, the student finds the stationary points of y = x3+ax2+b. 4a3 In step II, the student assumes that 27b b+ < 0, which is the correct thing to do to prove an ‘if ... then’ statement. In step III, the student says ‘if the cubic has three distinct real roots then ...’, which is correct but is not useful: we know that one of the stationary points is above the x-axis and the other is below and we wish to deduce that the cubic has three distinct real roots. In step IV, the student uses the result of II and the result ‘if one of the stationary points is above the x-axis and one is below, then the equation has three distinct real roots’, which is the converse of the statement in step III. Therefore the correct option is E: the student should have shown the converse of the result in step III.

questions.text: P, Q, R and S show the graphs of y = (cos x) cos x, y = (sin x) sin x, y = (cos x) sin x and y = (sin x) cos x for 0 < x < π 2 in some order. P 0 6 π 0.5 1 3 π 2 π Q 0 6 π 0.5 1 3 π 2 π R 0 6 π 0.5 1 3 π 2 π S 0 6 π 0.5 1 3 π 2 π Which row in the following table correctly identifies the graphs? y = (cos x) cos x y = (sin x) sin x y = (cos x) sin x y = (sin x) cos x
questions.workedSolution: Let us substitute values into the four equations to begin with. When x = 0, we have (cosx)cosx = 11 = 1 (sinx)sinx = 00 = ? (cosx)sinx = 10 = 1 (sinx)cosx = 01 = 0 This shows that graph Q is y = (sinx)cosx. (We do not know what (sinx)sinx is when x = 0, but from the graphs given, since three of the functions have value 1 at x = 0 and only one is 0, it must be 1.) Next, let us consider the value at x = π: (cosx)cosx = 00 = ? (sinx)sinx = 11 = 1 (cosx)sinx = 01 = 0 (sinx)cosx = 10 = 1 Again, as only one graph has value 0 at x = π, graph P depicts (cosx)sinx. (We did not actually need to calculate the final line of this list, as we have already identified this function as being graph Q.) So we are left with (cosx)cosx and (sinx)sinx as graphs R and S in some order. Let us put in the value x = π, as that is clearly different between the two functions. We have √ √ 3 3 2 (cosx)cosx = 1 2 (sinx)sinx = The first of these looks horrible, but we can approximate the value of the second of these; it is just √1 ≈ 0.7, so this must be graph S. Similarly, (cosx)cosx ≈ 0.7 at x = π, which is graph R. 2 3 Therefore the graphs are: (cosx)cosx : graph R (sinx)sinx : graph S (cosx)sinx : graph P (sinx)cosx : graph Q which is option E.

questions.text: A polygon has n vertices, where n  3. It has the following properties: • Every vertex of the polygon lies on the circumference of a circle C. • The centre of the circle C is inside the polygon. • The radii from the centre of the circle C to the vertices of the polygon cut the polygon into n triangles of equal area. For which values of n are these properties sufficient to deduce that the polygon is regular?
questions.workedSolution: We sketch a part of the polygon with the triangles described in the question: θ Suppose the angle of one of the triangles is θ, as shown. Then the area of the triangle is given by 1r2sinθ where r is the radius of the circle (using the formula A = 1absinC). 2 2 Since r is the same for each of the triangles, all the triangles will have equal area if and only if sinθ is the same for each triangle. This will certainly be the case if the polygon is regular, but we have to determine if this can be true in any other situation. If two of the triangles have angles at the centre of the circle of θ and φ, then sinφ = sinθ if and only if φ = θ or φ = π −θ. Can we have the latter possibility? If so, it would mean we have some angles being θ and some being π−θ. Suppose then that θ is acute and π−θ is obtuse. (We cannot have a reflex angle as the centre of the circle lies inside the polygon, and if θ is a right angle, then π−θ = θ.) Suppose further that k of the angles of the polygon triangles equal π−θ and the remaining n−k equal θ. Then the sum of these triangle angles is given by (n−k)θ+k(π−θ) = 2π since they form a whole circle. We can rearrange this to (n−2k)θ = (2−k)π and hence (2−k)π θ = . n−2k There can be at most three obtuse angled triangles (as four would be more than a whole circle), so let’s try k = 1, k = 2 and k = 3 in turn. If k = 1, we have θ = π . It looks as though this will work for every n, but we have to be n−2 a little careful as we need θ to be acute. When n = 3, we get θ = π, which is not allowed. When n = 4, we get θ = π, which is again not allowed. When n > 4, θ < π, so this does work. 2 2 Therefore when n ≥ 5, it is possible for the conditions to be satisfied but for the polygon not to be regular. Now consider k = 2, giving θ = 0 . We only need to consider n = 3 and n = 4, as we have n−4 dealtwithn ≥ 5above. Thisiszerowhenn = 3, soitdoesnotworkforatriangle. Whenn = 4, this formula is not meaningful (it gives 0). We can instead go back to the original equation (n−2k)θ = (2−k)π, which becomes 0 = 0 in this case, meaning that it works for any value of θ. And indeed, this gives two angles of θ and two of π−θ, so we get a non-regular polygon with equal area triangles. The simplest example is a non-square rectangle. We are left with the case k = 3 to consider. When n = 3, there would be no acute angles and three obtuse ones, and the formula gives θ = −π = π. And indeed, a triangle with three −3 3

questions.text: The functions f1 to f5 are defined on the real numbers by f1(x) = cos x f2(x) = sin (cos x) f3(x) = cos (sin (cos x)) f4(x) = sin (cos (sin (cos x))) f5(x) = cos (sin (cos (sin (cos x)))) where all numbers are taken to be in radians. These functions have maximum values m 1, m 2, m 3, m 4 and m 5 , respectively. Which one of the following statements is true?

options.A: m 1, m 2, m 3, m 4 and m 5 are all equal to 1

questions.workedSolution: We note that we can rewrite each of the functions f to f in terms of the previous function: 2 5 f (x) = cosx f (x) = sinf (x) 2 1 f (x) = cosf (x) 3 2 f (x) = sinf (x) 4 3 f (x) = cosf (x) 5 4 Since f (x) = cosx is periodic with period 2π, each of the other functions also repeats every 2π (thoughitmayhaveashortestperiodlessthanthis),andsoweonlyneedtoconsider0 ≤ x ≤ 2π. (Wecouldactuallyrestrictourselvesto0 ≤ x ≤ π, asthevaluesofcosxintherangeπ < x ≤ 2π are a repeat of the values in the range 0 ≤ x ≤ π, but we will draw a complete period anyway.) Sketching the graph of each of these functions is the most straightforward way to proceed. We note first a result that we will need later: π ≈ 3.14 so π ≈ 1.6 and hence sin1 < sin π = 1. 2 2 A sketch of f (x) = cosx is straightforward: y x −1 We see that m = 1, and the minimum value taken is −1. Now f (x) = sinf (x), so this goes 1 2 1 between −sin1 and sin1, with roots where f (x) = 0; recall that sin1 < 1: y x −1 We see that m = sin1 < 1, and the minimum value taken is −sin1.

questions.text: Given that d ax b x + = ∫ 0( ) and d x ax b x + = ∫ 0 ( ) find the value of a + b.
questions.workedSolution: We calculate the integrals in terms of a and b: (ax+b)dx = 1ax2+bx 1 2 0 = 1a+b = 1 1 1 x(ax+b)dx = (ax2+bx)dx 0 0 = 1ax3+ 1bx21 3 2 0 = 1a+ 1b 3 2 = 1 Multiplying the first equation by 2 and the second by 6 gives the simultaneous equations a+2b = 2 (1) 2a+3b = 6 (2) Then2×(1)−(2)givesb = −2, soa = 6. Thereforea+b = 4andthecorrectanswerisoptionF.

questions.text: The graphs of y = x 2 + 5 x + 6 and y = m x – 3, where m is a constant, are plotted on the same set of axes. Given that the graphs do not meet, what is the complete range of possible values of m?
questions.workedSolution: As the graphs do not meet, the simultaneous equations y = x2 +5x+6 and y = mx−3 have no solutions. Therefore x2+5x+6 = mx−3 has no solutions, so the quadratic x2+(5−m)x+9 = 0 has no solutions and must therefore have a negative discriminant. This gives (5−m)2−4×1×9 = 25−10m+m2−36 = m2−10m−11 < 0. Factorising this quadratic in m gives (m−11)(m+1) < 0. This quadratic has roots −1 and 11, and as the coefficient of m2 in m2−10m−11 is positive, the quadratic in m is negative between the roots. So the complete range of possible values of m is −1 < m < 11, which is option A.

questions.text: For any integer n ≥ 0 , d n n f x x n + = + ∫ 1 ( ) 1 Evaluate d d d d d f x x f x x f x x f x x f x x + + + + ∫ ∫ ∫ ∫ ∫ 0 1 2 4 ( ) ( ) ( ) ( ) ( )
questions.workedSolution: We work out the values of each of the individual integrals by splitting them into unit intervals: 3 1 2 3 f(x)dx = f(x)dx+ f(x)dx+ f(x)dx 0 0 1 2 = 1+2+3 = 6 3 2 3 f(x)dx = f(x)dx+ f(x)dx 1 1 2 = 2+3 = 5 f(x)dx = 3 3 4 f(x)dx = − f(x)dx 4 3 = −4 3 5 f(x)dx = − f(x)dx 5 3 4 5 = − f(x)dx+ f(x)dx 3 4 = −(4+5) = −9 b a where for the final two integrals, we have used the rule f(x)dx = − f(x)dx. a b Adding these gives the required sum: 6+5+3−4−9 = 1 which is option C.

questions.text: Evaluate n= ∞ ∑ 0 n n π ⎛ ⎞ π + ⎜ ⎟ ⎝ ⎠ 3 2 sin
questions.workedSolution: To help us understand what is going on here, we will work out the first few terms. ∞ sin nπ+ π sin(π sin(π+ π sin(2π+ π sin(3π+ π 3 = 3 + 3 + 3 + 3 +··· 2n 20 21 22 23 n=0 √ √ √ √ 3/2 − 3/2 3/2 − 3/2 = + + + +··· 1 2 4 8 √ 3 1 1 1 1 = − + − +··· 2 1 2 4 8 We see that the part in the brackets is a geometric series with first term a = 1 and common ratio r = −1, so its sum is a 1 1 2 = = = 1−r 1−(−1) 3/2 3 and hence the sum of the original series is √ √ 3 2 3 × = 2 3 3 which is option C.

questions.text: The following shape has two lines of reflectional symmetry. N S O T P R M U [diagram not to scale] MNOP is a square of perimeter 40 cm. The vertices of rectangle RSTU lie on the edge of square MNOP. MR has length x cm. What is the largest possible value of x such that RSTU has area 20 cm2 ?
questions.workedSolution: There are (at least) two ways to calculate the area of the rectangle RSTU: we could find the lengths of the sides RU and RS and then multiply them, or we could find the area of the square MNOP and subtract the areas of the shaded triangles. We demonstrate both approaches. We ignore the units, taking lengths to be in cm and areas to be in cm2. We first note that the symmetry of the shape means that MU = MR, NR = NS and so on. Also, as the perimeter of MNOP is 40, the side length is 10, so NR = 10−x. Approach 1: Finding the lengths of the rectangle’s sides ThetriangleRMU isright-angled, withMR = MU = x. ByPythagoras’sTheorem, thismeans √ that RU2 = x2+x2 = 2x2, so RU = x 2. √ Now NR = 10−x, so by a similar argument, RS = (10−x) 2. √ √ Multiplying these gives the area as A = x 2×(10−x) 2 = 2x(10−x). Approach 2: Finding the area by subtraction The area of MNOP is 102 = 100. The area of MRU and the area of OST are each 1x2, so the total area of these two triangles is x2. Likewise, the area of NRS and the area of PTU are each 1(10−x)2, so the total area of these two triangles is (10−x)2. Therefore the area of the rectangle RSTU is 100−x2−(10−x)2 = 100−x2−(100−20x+x2) = 20x−2x2 = 2x(10−x). We are told that A = 20, so we have to solve the equation 2x(10−x) = 20. Expanding and rearranging gives 2x2−20x+20 = 0; dividing by 2 simplifies this to x2−10x+10 = 0. We can use the quadratic formula to find the possible values of x; they are √ 10± (−10)2−4×1×10 10± 60 √ x = = = 5± 15. 2 2 √ The larger of these is 5+ 15, and so the answer is option F.

questions.text: In the simplified expansion of (2 + 3x)12, how many of the terms have a coefficient that is divisible by 12?
questions.workedSolution: A coefficient is divisible by 12 if and only if it is a multiple of 22 = 4 and a multiple of 3. The coefficient of xk (for 0 ≤ k ≤ 12) is given by 12 212−k3k. k This is certainly a multiple of 3 when k ≥ 1. This is certainly a multiple of 22 = 4 when k ≤ 10. We therefore only need to check k = 0, k = 11 and k = 12. When k = 0, the coefficient is 12 ×212 = 212, which is not a multiple of 3, and so not of 12 either. When k = 11, the coefficient is 12 ×2×311. But 12 = 12 = 12, so this is a multiple of 12. 11 11 1 When k = 12, the coefficient is 12 ×312 = 312, which is not a multiple of 2, and so not of 12 either. Therefore the coefficient is a multiple of 12 for 1 ≤ k ≤ 11, so 11 terms have such a coefficient, and the answer is option E.

questions.text: P(x) and Q(x) are defined as follows: P(x) = 2 x + 4 Q(x) = 2( 2 x – 2) – 2( x + 2) + 16 Find the largest value of x such that P(x) and Q(x) are in the ratio 4 : 1, respectively.
questions.workedSolution: Note that P(x) and Q(x) being in the ratio 4 : 1 means that P(x) = 4Q(x), so 2x+4 = 4 2(2x−2)−2(x+2)+16 . Simplifying the powers, noting that 4 = 22, gives 2x+4 = 22x−2x+4+64. This is a quadratic in 2x, so let us write u = 2x. The equation can then be written as u+4 = u2−16u+64. This rearranges to u2−17u+60 = 0, which factorises as (u−5)(u−12) = 0, so u = 5 or u = 12. Therefore, as u = 2x, we have x = log 5 or x = log 12, and the largest possible value of x is 2 2 log 12, which is option F.

questions.text: A triangle XYZ is called fun if it has the following properties: angle YXZ = 30° XY = 3 a YZ = a where a is a constant. For a given value of a, there are two distinct fun triangles S and T, where the area of S is greater than the area of T. Find the ratio area of S : area of T
questions.workedSolution: Let us sketch a fun triangle. Y √ 3a a 30° X Z √ The area of XYZ is given by the ‘1absinC’ formula: it is 1XY.XZsin30° = 1 3a.XZ. We 2 2 4 must therefore find the possible values of XZ. We can use the cosine rule to find XZ: we have YZ2 = XY2+XZ2−2XY.XZcos30°. Filling in the known values, we obtain a2 = 3a2+XZ2−3a.XZ so XZ satisfies the quadratic equation XZ2−3a.XZ +2a2 = 0. This factorises to give (XZ −2a)(XZ −a) = 0 so either XZ = a or XZ = 2a. It is useful to think about what the two resulting triangles look like and whether these values of XZ make sense. We are asked to find the ratio of the areas of these possible fun triangles. Since the area is given √ by 1 3a.XZ, the ratio of areas is given by the ratio of possible XZ values. Since triangle S has the larger area, it must have the larger of the XZ values, so the ratio is 2a : a, which equals 2 : 1. The correct answer is thus option B. Commentary: There are lots of appearances of XY, YZ and XZ here, following the language used in the question. It might have been simpler to use the standard side-length naming convention, letting x be the length of the side YZ opposite X, letting y be the length of the side XZ opposite Y and z the length of XY. Then we would have the area √ of XYZ being 1yzsin30° = 1 3ay, the cosine rule would read 2 4 x2 = y2+z2−2yzcos30°, and we would finally deduce that y = a or y = 2a.

questions.text: How many solutions are there to (1 + 3 cos 3 θ )2 = 4 in the interval 0° ≤ θ ≤ 180° ?
questions.workedSolution: We have (1 + 3cos3θ)2 = 4 if and only if 1 + 3cos3θ = ±2. We consider each possibility separately. We have 1+3cos3θ = 2 if and only if 3cos3θ = 1 if and only if cos3θ = 1. Since 0° ≤ θ ≤ 180°, we have 0° ≤ 3θ ≤ 540°, and there are 3 values of 3θ which have cos3θ = 1 in this interval. (One is between 0° and 90°, one is between 270° and 360°, and one is between 360° and 450°, by considering the graph of y = cosx.) Now considering the other possibility, we have 1+3cos3θ = −2 if and only if 3cos3θ = −3 if and only if cos3θ = −1. Again, 0° ≤ 3θ ≤ 540°, but this time there are only 2 values of 3θ which satisfy the equation: 3θ = 180° and 3θ = 540°. Neither of these values of 3θ overlap with the values of 3θ found earlier, so in total there are 3 + 2 = 5 values of 3θ in the interval 0° ≤ 3θ ≤ 540°, and hence 5 solutions to the original equation in the given interval. The correct answer is option E.

questions.text: [Question 10 text - see source PDF: https://uat-wp.s3.eu-west-2.amazonaws.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/30144109/TMUA-2023-paper-1.pdf]
questions.workedSolution: Though integrating functions like this is not on the syllabus, the question assumes that we can calculate the value of this integral. There is also a hint from the options offered that it may have something to do with circles, as they all involve π. √ Let us therefore consider the graph y = 4−x2. Squaring this gives y2 = 4−x2 or x2+y2 = 4. This is the equation of a circle with centre at the origin and radius 2. However, as the graph involves taking the square root of this, the resulting graph is just the upper semicircle (on and above the x-axis). y −2 2 x The integral is from x = −2 to x = 2, which is the whole diameter, so the integral is the area of the semicircle, that is, 1 ×π×22 = 2π. We now need to calculate the area as determined by the trapezium rule with 4 strips. We can draw a table of values: x −2 −1 0 1 2 √ √ √ 4−x2 0 3 2 3 0 The trapezium rule then approximates the area as √ √ √ √ 1 ×1× 0+2× 3+2×2+2× 3+0 = 1 × 4+4 3 = 2+2 3. 2 2 The positive difference (as the trapezium rule underestimates the area) is therefore √ √ 2π− 2+2 3 = 2 π−1− 3 which is option B.

questions.text: It is given that f (x) = x2 – 6x The curves y = f (k x) and y = f (x – c) have the same minimum point, where k > 0 and c > 0 Which of the following is a correct expression for k in terms of c ?
questions.workedSolution: We first find the minimum points of the two graphs. Approach 1: Direct approach We have, for the first graph, y = f(kx) = (kx)2−6(kx) = k2x2−6kx. We can find the minimum point using either calculus or completing the square; we will use calculus here. We have dy = 2k2x−6k dx so dy = 0 exactly when x = 3. At this point, y = k2(3)2−6k(3) = −9. dx k k k For the second graph, we have y = f(x−c) = (x−c)2−6(x−c) = x2−2cx+c2−6x+6c = x2−(6+2c)x+c2+6c. We use completing the square to find the minimum point: we have y = (x−(3+c))2−(3+c)2+c2+6c = (x−(3+c))2−9 so the minimum point is at x = 3+c, y = −9. Approach 2: Using graph transformations We first find the minimum point of y = x2 − 6x. We can complete the square to get y = (x−3)2−9, so the minimum point is at x = 3, y = −9. The graph y = f(kx) is obtained by stretching the original graph by a factor of 1/k in the x-direction, so the minimum of this graph is at x = 3/k, y = −9. The graph y = f(x−c) is obtainedd by translating the original graph by c in the x-direction, so the minimum of this graph is at x = 3+c, y = −9. For the two minima to be at the same place, we require 3/k = 3+c, so taking the reciprocal gives k 1 = . 3 3+c Multiplying by 3 then gives k = , 3+c which is option B.

questions.text: How many solutions are there to the equation x x = 2 2 2 1 4 tan sin in the range 0 ≤ x ≤ 2π ?
questions.workedSolution: Let us start by multiplying both sides by 4sin2x to remove the fraction: 2tan2x = 4sin2x. We note that 4a = (22)a = 22a, so we can rewrite this equation as 2tan2x = 22sin2x, and therefore the equation can be simplified to tan2x = 2sin2x. Next, we know that tanx = sinx, so we can rewrite this as cosx sin2x = 2sin2x. cos2x Multiplying by cos2x to eliminate fractions once more gives sin2x = 2sin2xcos2x, which we can rearrange and factorise to give sin2x(1−2cos2x) = 0. Therefore either sinx = 0 or cos2x = 1. The first has solutions x = 0, π, 2π. The second gives cosx = ±√1 , so has 4 solutions in the range 0 ≤ x ≤ 2π, none of which are 0, π or 2π. (We could list them explicitly: they are π, 3π, 5π, 7π.) 4 4 4 4 In total, there are 7 solutions, which is option F.

questions.text: Point P lies on the circle with equation (x – 2)2 + (y – 1)2 = 16 Point Q lies on the circle with equation (x – 4)2 + (y + 5)2 = 16 What is the maximum possible length of PQ ?
questions.workedSolution: It would help to sketch the circles. The first circle has centre (2,1) and radius 4; the second has centre (4,−5) and radius 4. (This is not an accurate sketch, though we have noted that the second circle touches the y-axis.) y (2,1) x (4,−5) It is clear that the furthest apart that P and Q could be is if they are on the line that passes through the two circle centres as shown below: y P (2,1) x (4,−5) Q The distance between P and Q is then the sum of the radius of the first circle, the distance between the centres and the radius of the second circle. √ √ The distance between the centres is (4−2)2+(−5−1)2 = 40 = 2 10 and each radius is 4, √ so the maximum distance is 8+2 10, which is option F.

questions.text: The function f (x) = x 3 2 3 + 2mx2 + n, m > 0 has three distinct real roots. What is the complete range of possible values of n, in terms of m?
questions.workedSolution: The function will have three distinct real roots if and only if the graph y = f(x) has two stationary points, one above the x-axis and one below. We first find the stationary points. We have f(x) = 2x2+4mx = 2x(x+2m) so f(x) = 0 when x = 0 or x = −2m. When x = 0, f(x) = n, and when x = −2m, 2 8 f(x) = (−8m3)+2m(4m2)+n = n+ m3. 3 3 Since one of the stationary points has f(x) = n, we cannot have n = 0. Since m > 0, it follows that if n > 0, then f(−2m) > 0 and both turning points would be above the x-axis. We must therefore have n < 0 and n+ 8m3 > 0. The second inequality rearranges to −8m3 < n, so we require −8m3 < n < 0, which is option A. 3 3

questions.text: The difference between the maximum and minimum values of the function f (x) = a cos x, where a > 0 and x is real, is 3. Find the sum of the possible values of a.
questions.workedSolution: The minimum and maximum values occur when cosx = ±1 (though which corresponds to the maximum and which to the minimum depends on whether a < 1 or a > 1). Therefore the minimum and maximum values are a and 1/a in some order. Assuming a > 1, so that a > 1/a, we require a− 1 = 3. Multiplying by a and rearranging gives √ a a2 −3a−1 = 0, which has roots a = 3± 13. The minus sign would give a < 0, so the root we √ 2 want is a = 3+ 13, and this clearly satisfies a > 1. Now assuming a < 1, so that 1/a > a, we require 1 −a = 3. Multiplying by a and rearranging √a gives a2 +3a−1 = 0, which has roots a = −3± 13. The minus sign would give a < 0, so the √ √ 2 root we want is a = −3+ 13 (and as 3 < 13 < 4, this lies between 0 and 1, hence a < 1). 2 2 √ √ √ The sum of these two values is 3+ 13 + −3+ 13 = 13, which is option F. 2 2

questions.text: A right-angled triangle has vertices at (2, 3), (9, –1) and (5, k ). Find the sum of all the possible values of k.
questions.workedSolution: A sketch is useful here; we also label the points as A(2,3), B(9,−1) and C(5,k).
